cue: How do you say someone is nobody's fool?

es.meaning: To be shrewd; to be nobody's fool.
es.example: A ese no lo engañas: sabe latín.
es.exampleTranslation: You won't fool him — he's nobody's fool.

it.meaning: To be shrewd; to know more than one lets on.
it.example: Quello non lo freghi: la sa lunga.
it.exampleTranslation: You won't fool him — he's nobody's fool.

bridge.note: Two ways of measuring cunning. Spanish measures it by CONTENT: knowing Latin, the old marker of a real education, so nobody can dazzle you with what they know. Italian measures it by EXTENT: la sa lunga, they know it LONG — knowledge stretching further than the conversation lets on. Depth of learning versus reach of knowing, and only the Italian implies the person is holding something back.

interference.es_to_it: 'Sapere il latino' means literally knowing the language, nothing more. Shrewdness is saperLA lunga — note the obligatory la.
interference.it_to_es: 'Saberla larga' is not Spanish; cunning there is classical education: saber latín.

review.notes: ES side discovered in the Spanish idioms book Joe is reading, 2026-07-21 (discovery only; all glosses and examples authored fresh). IT counterpart from Claude's recall — UNVERIFIED. Content-vs-extent framing; the Italian la is a small grammar lesson in itself.
